The **Muslim period in the Indian subcontinent** or **Indo-Muslim period**[[1]](#cite_note-1) is conventionally said to have started in 640 with the [conquest of Makran](/wiki/Battle_of_Rasil "Battle of Rasil") by the [Rashidun Caliphate](/wiki/Rashidun_Caliphate "Rashidun Caliphate") and was continued in 712–714, after the [conquest of Sindh](/wiki/Arab_conquest_of_Sindh "Arab conquest of Sindh"), [Taxila](/wiki/Taxila "Taxila") and [Multan](/wiki/Conquest_of_Multan_(714) "Conquest of Multan (714)") by the [Umayyad Caliphate](/wiki/Umayyad_Caliphate "Umayyad Caliphate") under the military command of [Muhammad ibn al-Qasim](/wiki/Muhammad_ibn_al-Qasim "Muhammad ibn al-Qasim").[[2]](#cite_note-2) It began in the [Indian subcontinent](/wiki/Indian_subcontinent "Indian subcontinent") in the course of a [gradual conquest](/wiki/Muslim_conquest_in_the_Indian_subcontinent "Muslim conquest in the Indian subcontinent"). The perfunctory rule by the [Ghaznavids](/wiki/Ghaznavids "Ghaznavids") in [Punjab](/wiki/Punjab "Punjab") was followed by [Ghurids](/wiki/Ghurids "Ghurids"), and [Sultan Muhammad of Ghor](/wiki/Muhammad_of_Ghor "Muhammad of Ghor") (r. 1173–1206) is generally credited with laying the foundation of Muslim rule in Northern India. Muslim rule in the Indian subcontinent also led to major developments in architecture, including the introduction of Persian-influenced designs, arches, domes, and decorative calligraphy. These styles shaped many of the region's most iconic structures and contributed to the formation of Indo-Islamic architecture.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

From the late 12th century onwards, Muslim empires dominated the subcontinent, most notably the [Delhi Sultanate](/wiki/Delhi_Sultanate "Delhi Sultanate") and [Mughal Empire](/wiki/Mughal_Empire "Mughal Empire").[[4]](#cite_note-4) Various other Muslim kingdoms ruled most of [South Asia](/wiki/South_Asia "South Asia") from the mid-14th to late 18th centuries, including the [Bahmani](/wiki/Bahmani_Sultanate "Bahmani Sultanate"), [Bengal](/wiki/Bengal_Sultanate "Bengal Sultanate"), [Gujarat](/wiki/Gujarat_Sultanate "Gujarat Sultanate"), [Malwa](/wiki/Malwa_Sultanate "Malwa Sultanate"), [Kashmir](/wiki/Kashmir_Sultanate "Kashmir Sultanate"), [Multan](/wiki/Sultanate_of_Multan "Sultanate of Multan"), [Mysore](/wiki/Mysore_Sultanate "Mysore Sultanate"), [Carnatic](/wiki/Carnatic_Sultanate "Carnatic Sultanate"), [Arakkal](/wiki/Arakkal_kingdom "Arakkal kingdom") and [Deccan Sultanates](/wiki/Deccan_sultanates "Deccan sultanates").[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-Lane-Poole1991-6) Though the Muslim dynasties in India were diverse in origin, they were linked together by the [Persianate culture](/wiki/Persianate_culture "Persianate culture") and Islam.

The height of Islamic rule was marked during the reign of [Mughal Emperor](/wiki/Mughal_Emperor "Mughal Emperor") [Aurangzeb](/wiki/Aurangzeb "Aurangzeb") (r. 1658–1707), during which the [Fatawa Alamgiri](/wiki/Fatwa_Alamgiri "Fatwa Alamgiri") was compiled, which briefly served as the legal system of Mughal Empire.[[7]](#cite_note-7) Additional Islamic policies were re-introduced in [South India](/wiki/South_India "South India") by Mysore's de facto king [Tipu Sultan](/wiki/Tipu_Sultan "Tipu Sultan").[[8]](#cite_note-pande-8)

[Sharia](/wiki/Sharia "Sharia") was used as the primary basis for the legal system in the Delhi Sultanate, most notably during the rule of [Firuz Shah Tughlaq](/wiki/Firuz_Shah_Tughlaq "Firuz Shah Tughlaq") and [Alauddin Khilji](/wiki/Alauddin_Khilji "Alauddin Khilji"), who repelled the [Mongol invasions of India](/wiki/Mongol_invasions_of_India "Mongol invasions of India"). On the other hand, rulers such as [Akbar](/wiki/Akbar "Akbar") adopted a secular legal system and enforced religious neutrality.[[9]](#cite_note-9) Muslim rule in India saw a major shift in the cultural, linguistic, and religious makeup of the subcontinent.[[10]](#cite_note-10) [Persian](/wiki/Persian_language "Persian language") and [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic "Arabic") vocabulary began to enter local languages, giving way to modern Punjabi, Bengali, and Gujarati, while creating new languages including [Hindustani](/wiki/Hindustani_language "Hindustani language") and its dialect, [Deccani](/wiki/Deccani_Urdu "Deccani Urdu"), used as official languages under Muslim dynasties.[[11]](#cite_note-11) This period also saw the birth of [Hindustani music](/wiki/Hindustani_music "Hindustani music"), [Qawwali](/wiki/Qawwali "Qawwali").[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13) Religions such as [Sikhism](/wiki/Sikhism "Sikhism") and [Din-e-Ilahi](/wiki/Din-i_Ilahi "Din-i Ilahi") were born out of a fusion of Hindu and Muslim religious traditions as well.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

In the 18th century the Islamic influence in India begin to decline following the [decline of the Mughal Empire](/wiki/Decline_of_the_Mughal_Empire "Decline of the Mughal Empire"), resulting in former Mughal territory conquered by the rival powers such as the [Maratha Empire](/wiki/Maratha_Empire "Maratha Empire"). However, Islamic rule would still remain under regional [Nawabs](/wiki/Nawabs "Nawabs") and [Sultans](/wiki/Sultan "Sultan").

However, Marathas still used a [Islamic-style of administration](/wiki/Durbar_(court) "Durbar (court)") and many Muslims were also in the Maratha administration. Throughout the 18th and 19th centuries, large parts of India were colonized by the [East India Company](/wiki/British_East_India_Company "British East India Company"), eventually establishing the [British Raj](/wiki/British_Raj "British Raj") in 1857. Regional Islamic rule would remain under [princely states](/wiki/Princely_state "Princely state"), such as [Hyderabad State](/wiki/Hyderabad_State "Hyderabad State"), [Junagadh State](/wiki/Junagadh_State "Junagadh State"), and other minor [princely states](/wiki/Princely_state "Princely state") until the mid of the 20th century.

Today, [Bangladesh](/wiki/Bangladesh "Bangladesh"), [Maldives](/wiki/Maldives "Maldives") and [Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan "Pakistan") are the Muslim majority nations in the Indian subcontinent while [India](/wiki/India "India") has the largest [Muslim](/wiki/Muslim "Muslim") minority population in the world numbering over 204 million.

### Early Muslim dominions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Muslim_period_in_the_Indian_subcontinent&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Early Muslim dominions")]

See also: [Muslim conquests in the Indian subcontinent](/wiki/Muslim_conquests_in_the_Indian_subcontinent "Muslim conquests in the Indian subcontinent") and [Umayyad campaigns in India](/wiki/Umayyad_campaigns_in_India "Umayyad campaigns in India")

Local kings who converted to [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam") existed in places such as the [Western Coastal Plains](/wiki/Western_Coastal_Plains "Western Coastal Plains") as early as the 7th century. Islamic rule in India prior to the advent of the [Mamluk dynasty (Delhi)](/wiki/Mamluk_dynasty_(Delhi) "Mamluk dynasty (Delhi)") included those of [Arab Caliphate](/wiki/Arab_Sind "Arab Sind"), [Ghaznavids](/wiki/Ghaznavid "Ghaznavid") and [Ghurids](/wiki/Ghurid "Ghurid").

### Delhi Sultanate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Muslim_period_in_the_Indian_subcontinent&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Delhi Sultanate")]

Main article: [Delhi Sultanate](/wiki/Delhi_Sultanate "Delhi Sultanate")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/72/Map_of_the_Delhi_Sultanate.png/250px-Map_of_the_Delhi_Sultanate.png)](/wiki/File:Map_of_the_Delhi_Sultanate.png)

[Delhi Sultanate](/wiki/Delhi_Sultanate "Delhi Sultanate") in 1330-1335 during [Tughlaq era](/wiki/Tughlaq_dynasty "Tughlaq dynasty")

The Delhi Sultanate was the first of the two major Islamic empires which was based in mainland India between 1206 and 1526. It emerged after the disintegration of the [Ghurid empire](/wiki/Ghurid_Empire "Ghurid Empire") in 1206. During the last quarter of the 12th century, [Muhammad of Ghor](/wiki/Muhammad_of_Ghor "Muhammad of Ghor") invaded the [Indo-Gangetic plain](/wiki/Indo-Gangetic_plain "Indo-Gangetic plain"), conquering in succession [Ghazni](/wiki/Ghazni "Ghazni"), [Multan](/wiki/Multan "Multan"), [Lahore](/wiki/Lahore "Lahore"), and [Delhi](/wiki/Delhi "Delhi"). [Qutb-ud-din Aybak](/wiki/Qutb-ud-din_Aybak "Qutb-ud-din Aybak"), one of his generals proclaimed himself [Sultan of Delhi](/wiki/Sultan_of_Delhi "Sultan of Delhi"). In [Bengal](/wiki/Bengal "Bengal") and [Bihar](/wiki/Bihar "Bihar"), the reign of general [Muhammad bin Bakhtiyar Khalji](/wiki/Muhammad_bin_Bakhtiyar_Khalji "Muhammad bin Bakhtiyar Khalji") was established. [Shamsuddīn Iltutmish](/wiki/Iltutmish "Iltutmish") (1211–1236), established the Delhi Sultanate on a firm basis, which enabled future sultans to push in every direction. Within the next 100 years, the Delhi Sultanate extended its way east to [Bengal](/wiki/Bengal "Bengal") and south to the [Deccan](/wiki/Deccan_Plateau "Deccan Plateau"). The sultanate was in constant flux as five dynasties rose and fell: the [Mamluk dynasty](/wiki/Mamluk_dynasty_(Delhi) "Mamluk dynasty (Delhi)") (1206–90),[[15]](#cite_note-Gat-15) [Khalji dynasty](/wiki/Khalji_dynasty "Khalji dynasty") (1290–1320), [Tughlaq dynasty](/wiki/Tughlaq_dynasty "Tughlaq dynasty") (1320–1413), [Sayyid dynasty](/wiki/Sayyid_dynasty "Sayyid dynasty") (1414–51),[[16]](#cite_note-16) and [Lodi dynasty](/wiki/Lodi_dynasty "Lodi dynasty") (1451–1526).[[17]](#cite_note-17) Power in Delhi was often gained by violence—nineteen of the thirty-five sultans were assassinated—and was legitimized by reward for tribal loyalty. Factional rivalries and court intrigues were as numerous as they were treacherous; territories controlled by the sultan expanded and shrank depending on his personality and fortunes.

The Delhi sultanate peaked under [Muhammad bin Tughlaq](/wiki/Muhammad_bin_Tughlaq "Muhammad bin Tughlaq") in 1335. However, it came under gradual decline afterwards, with kingdoms like the [Bengal Sultanate](/wiki/Bengal_Sultanate "Bengal Sultanate"), [Madurai Sultanate](/wiki/Madurai_Sultanate "Madurai Sultanate"), [Khandesh Sultanate](/wiki/Farooqui_dynasty "Farooqui dynasty") and [Bahmani Sultanate](/wiki/Bahmani_Sultanate "Bahmani Sultanate") all asserting independence. Timur's invasion in 1398 only accelerated the process, and the [Gujarat Sultanate](/wiki/Gujarat_Sultanate "Gujarat Sultanate") and [Jaunpur Sultanate](/wiki/Jaunpur_Sultanate "Jaunpur Sultanate") broke away. Some of these kingdoms, such as Jaunpur, were again brought back under the Delhi Sultanate's control, although the rest remained independent from central rule until the conquests of the [Mughal Empire](/wiki/Mughal_Empire "Mughal Empire") in the 16th and 17th centuries.

Both the [Qur'an](/wiki/Qur%27an "Qur'an") and [sharia](/wiki/Sharia "Sharia") (Islamic law) provided the basis for enforcing Islamic administration over the independent [Hindu](/wiki/Hindu "Hindu") rulers. According to [Angus Maddison](/wiki/Angus_Maddison "Angus Maddison"), between the years 1000 and 1500, India's [GDP](/wiki/Gross_domestic_product "Gross domestic product"), of which the sultanates represented a significant part, grew by nearly 80%, to $60.5 billion; however, this growth was lower than India's GDP growth during the prior 1,000 years.[[18]](#cite_note-maddison379-18) Additionally, Maddison estimates that India's population grew by nearly 50% during the same period.[[19]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMadison2007376-19) The Delhi Sultanate period coincided with a greater use of mechanical technology in the Indian subcontinent. While India previously already had sophisticated agriculture, food crops, textiles, medicine, minerals, and metals, it was not as sophisticated as the [Islamic world](/wiki/Islamic_world "Islamic world") or [China](/wiki/China "China") in terms of mechanical technology.[[20]](#cite_note-20) Sultan 'Ala ud-Din made an attempt to reassess, systematize, and unify land revenues and urban taxes and to institute a highly centralized system of administration over his realm, but his efforts were abortive. Although agriculture in [North India](/wiki/North_India "North India") improved as a result of new canal construction and irrigation methods, including what came to be known as the [Persian](/wiki/Persian_Empire "Persian Empire") wheel, prolonged political instability and parasitic methods of tax collection brutalized the peasantry. Yet trade and a market economy, encouraged by the free-spending habits of the aristocracy, acquired new impetus both in India and overseas. Experts in metalwork, stonework and textile manufacture responded to the new patronage with enthusiasm. In this period [Persian language](/wiki/Persian_language "Persian language") and many Persian cultural aspects became dominant in the centers of power, as the rulers of the Delhi Sultanate had been thoroughly Persianized since the era of the Ghaznavids.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c8/Taj_Mahal_in_March_2004.jpg/250px-Taj_Mahal_in_March_2004.jpg)](/wiki/File:Taj_Mahal_in_March_2004.jpg)

The [Taj Mahal](/wiki/Taj_Mahal "Taj Mahal"), built by [Shah Jahan](/wiki/Shah_Jahan "Shah Jahan").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ed/Mughal_Empire%2C_1707.png/250px-Mughal_Empire%2C_1707.png)](/wiki/File:Mughal_Empire,_1707.png)

Mughal empire in 1707

### Mughal Empire

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Muslim_period_in_the_Indian_subcontinent&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Mughal Empire")]

Main article: [Mughal Empire](/wiki/Mughal_Empire "Mughal Empire")

The [Mughal empire](/wiki/Mughal_Empire "Mughal Empire") was the second & last major Islamic empire to assert dominance over most of the [Indian subcontinent](/wiki/Indian_subcontinent "Indian subcontinent") between 1526 and 1857. The empire was founded by the Turco-Mongol leader [Babur](/wiki/Babur "Babur") in 1526, when he defeated [Ibrahim Lodi](/wiki/Ibrahim_Lodi "Ibrahim Lodi"), the last ruler of the [Delhi Sultanate](/wiki/Delhi_Sultanate "Delhi Sultanate") at the [First Battle of Panipat](/wiki/First_Battle_of_Panipat "First Battle of Panipat"). [Babur](/wiki/Babur "Babur"), [Humayun](/wiki/Humayun "Humayun"), [Akbar](/wiki/Akbar "Akbar"), [Jahangir](/wiki/Jahangir "Jahangir"), [Shah Jahan](/wiki/Shah_Jahan "Shah Jahan"), and [Aurangzeb](/wiki/Aurangzeb "Aurangzeb") are known as the six great [Mughal Emperors](/wiki/Mughal_Emperors "Mughal Emperors"). Apart from the brief interruption of 16 Years by the Afghan [Sur Empire](/wiki/Sur_Empire "Sur Empire") between 1540 and 1556, the Mughals continued to rule in one form or other till 1857.

India was producing 24.5% of the world's manufacturing output up until 1750.[[22]](#cite_note-Williamson-22) Mughal economy has been described as a form of [proto-industrialization](/wiki/Proto-industrialization "Proto-industrialization"), like that of 18th-century Western Europe prior to the [Industrial Revolution](/wiki/Industrial_Revolution "Industrial Revolution").

After the death of Aurangzeb in 1707, the empire declined and reduced subsequently to the region in and around Old Delhi by 1757 to 1760. The decline of the Mughals in the 18th century provided opportunity for the [Nawabs of Oudh](/wiki/Nawabs_of_Oudh "Nawabs of Oudh") and Bengal as well as [Nizam of Hyderabad](/wiki/Nizam_of_Hyderabad "Nizam of Hyderabad") to become independent. The empire was formally dissolved by the [British Raj](/wiki/British_Raj "British Raj") after the [Indian Rebellion of 1857](/wiki/Indian_Rebellion_of_1857 "Indian Rebellion of 1857").

### Western and central India

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Muslim_period_in_the_Indian_subcontinent&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Western and central India")]

[Sultan Alauddin Khalji](/wiki/Alauddin_Khalji "Alauddin Khalji") (r.1296–1316) carried out extensive conquests in the western India. He invaded the kingdoms of [Gujarat](/wiki/Alauddin_Khalji%27s_conquest_of_Gujarat "Alauddin Khalji's conquest of Gujarat") (raided in 1299 and annexed in 1304), [Jaisalmer](/wiki/Jaisalmer_Fort "Jaisalmer Fort") (1299), [Ranthambore](/wiki/Alauddin_Khalji%27s_conquest_of_Ranthambore "Alauddin Khalji's conquest of Ranthambore") (1301), [Chittor](/wiki/Siege_of_Chittorgarh_(1303) "Siege of Chittorgarh (1303)") (1303), [Malwa](/wiki/Alauddin_Khalji%27s_conquest_of_Malwa "Alauddin Khalji's conquest of Malwa") (1305), [Siwana](/wiki/Siege_of_Siwana "Siege of Siwana") (1308), and [Jalore](/wiki/Alauddin_Khalji%27s_conquest_of_Jalore "Alauddin Khalji's conquest of Jalore") (1311). These victories ended several [Rajput](/wiki/Rajput "Rajput") and other Hindu dynasties, including the [Paramaras](/wiki/Paramara_dynasty "Paramara dynasty"), the [Vaghelas](/wiki/Vaghela_dynasty "Vaghela dynasty"), the [Chahamanas of Ranastambhapura](/wiki/Chahamanas_of_Ranastambhapura "Chahamanas of Ranastambhapura") and [Jalore](/wiki/Chahamanas_of_Jalor "Chahamanas of Jalor"), the Rawal branch of the [Guhilas](/wiki/Guhila_dynasty "Guhila dynasty"), and possibly the [Yajvapalas](/wiki/Yajvapala_dynasty "Yajvapala dynasty"); and permanently establishing Muslim rule in the regions of central and western India. After his death, independent Islamic kingdoms emerged there.

#### Gujarat Sultanate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Muslim_period_in_the_Indian_subcontinent&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Gujarat Sultanate")]

The [Gujarat Sultanate](/wiki/Gujarat_Sultanate "Gujarat Sultanate") was founded by [Sultan Zafar Khan Muzaffar](/wiki/Muzaffar_Shah_I "Muzaffar Shah I"), whose ancestors were [Tāṅks](/wiki/T%C4%81%E1%B9%85ks "Tāṅks") from southern [Punjab](/wiki/Punjab "Punjab").[[23]](#cite_note-:1-23) Earlier, he was the governor of Gujarat appointed by the Tughlaq Sultans of Delhi. However, in the aftermath of the destruction of Delhi by Emir Timur, he declared independence in 1407. The next sultan, his grandson [Ahmad Shah I](/wiki/Ahmad_Shah_I "Ahmad Shah I") moved the capital to [Ahmedabad](/wiki/Ahmedabad "Ahmedabad") in 1411. His successor [Muhammad Shah II](/wiki/Muhammad_Shah_II "Muhammad Shah II") subdued most Rajput chieftains. The prosperity of the sultanate reached its zenith during the rule of [Mahmud Begada](/wiki/Mahmud_Begada "Mahmud Begada"). He also subdued most Gujarati Rajput chieftains and built a navy off the coast of [Diu](/wiki/Diu,_India "Diu, India"). In 1509, the [Portuguese empire](/wiki/Portuguese_empire "Portuguese empire") wrested Diu from the Sultanate in the [battle of Diu (1509)](/wiki/Battle_of_Diu_(1509) "Battle of Diu (1509)"). The [Moghul](/wiki/Mughal_Empire "Mughal Empire") emperor [Humayun](/wiki/Humayun "Humayun") attacked Gujarat in 1535 and briefly occupied it, during which [Bombay, Bassein & Damaon](/wiki/Portuguese_Bombay_and_Bassein "Portuguese Bombay and Bassein") would become a Portuguese colony, thereafter [Bahadur Shah](/wiki/Bahadur_Shah_of_Gujarat "Bahadur Shah of Gujarat") was killed by the Portuguese while making a deal in 1537. The end of the sultanate came in 1573, when [Akbar](/wiki/Akbar_the_Great "Akbar the Great") annexed Sultanate of Guzerat into his empire. The kingdom was primarily based in the present-day state of [Gujarat, India](/wiki/Gujarat,_India "Gujarat, India").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/57/India_in_1525_Joppen.jpg/250px-India_in_1525_Joppen.jpg)](/wiki/File:India_in_1525_Joppen.jpg)

Map of Indian subcontinent in 1525. Sultanates of [Gujarat](/wiki/Gujarat_Sultanate "Gujarat Sultanate"), [Malwa](/wiki/Malwa_Sultanate "Malwa Sultanate"), [Bengal](/wiki/Bengal_Sultanate "Bengal Sultanate"), [Kashmir](/wiki/Kashmir_Sultanate "Kashmir Sultanate") and [Delhi](/wiki/Delhi_Sultanate "Delhi Sultanate") as well as [Deccan sultanates](/wiki/Deccan_sultanates "Deccan sultanates") can be seen in the south.

#### Malwa Sultanate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Muslim_period_in_the_Indian_subcontinent&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Malwa Sultanate")]

The [Malwa Sultanate](/wiki/Malwa_Sultanate "Malwa Sultanate") was another Muslim kingdom in the [Malwa region](/wiki/Malwa "Malwa"), covering the present day [Indian states](/wiki/Indian_states "Indian states") of [Madhya Pradesh](/wiki/Madhya_Pradesh "Madhya Pradesh") and south-eastern [Rajasthan](/wiki/Rajasthan "Rajasthan") from 1392 to 1562. It was founded by [Dilawar Khan](/wiki/Dilawar_Khan "Dilawar Khan"), who following [Timur](/wiki/Timur "Timur")'s invasion and the disintegration of the Delhi Sultanate, in 1401/2, made Malwa an independent realm.[[24]](#cite_note-EncofIslam-24) In 1561, the Sultanate was conquered by the [Mughal empire](/wiki/Mughal_empire "Mughal empire") from its last ruler, [Baz Bahadur](/wiki/Baz_Bahadur "Baz Bahadur").

#### Other Western states

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Muslim_period_in_the_Indian_subcontinent&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Other Western states")]

[Sindh](/wiki/Sindh "Sindh") was ruled by a series of Muslim dynasties including [Habbaris](/wiki/Habbari_dynasty "Habbari dynasty"), [Soomras](/wiki/Soomra_dynasty "Soomra dynasty"), [Sammas](/wiki/Samma_dynasty "Samma dynasty"), [Arghuns](/wiki/Arghun_dynasty "Arghun dynasty") and [Tarkhans](/wiki/Tarkhan_dynasty "Tarkhan dynasty"), after the disintegration of Arab caliphate. Following decline of Mughal empire, [Kalhora](/wiki/Kalhora_dynasty "Kalhora dynasty") and [Talpur Nawabs](/wiki/Talpur_dynasty "Talpur dynasty") ruled Sindh. [Kingdom of Mewat](/wiki/Khanzadas_of_Mewat "Khanzadas of Mewat") was also a prominent Muslim Rajput kingdom in [Rajasthan](/wiki/Rajasthan "Rajasthan"). [Gonds of Deogarh](/wiki/Gonds_of_Deogarh "Gonds of Deogarh") was also a Gond/tribal Islamic kingdom located in [Nagpur](/wiki/Nagpur "Nagpur"), [Maharashtra](/wiki/Maharashtra "Maharashtra").

### North India

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Muslim_period_in_the_Indian_subcontinent&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: North India")]

#### Bengal Sultanate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Muslim_period_in_the_Indian_subcontinent&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Bengal Sultanate")]

Main article: [Bengal Sultanate](/wiki/Bengal_Sultanate "Bengal Sultanate")

In 1339, the Bengal region became independent from the [Delhi Sultanate](/wiki/Delhi_Sultanate "Delhi Sultanate") and consisted of numerous Islamic city-states. The [Bengal Sultanate](/wiki/Bengal_Sultanate "Bengal Sultanate") was formed in 1352 after [Shamsuddin Ilyas Shah](/wiki/Shamsuddin_Ilyas_Shah "Shamsuddin Ilyas Shah"), ruler of [Satgaon](/wiki/Satgaon "Satgaon"), defeated [Alauddin Ali Shah](/wiki/Alauddin_Ali_Shah "Alauddin Ali Shah") of Lakhnauti and [Ikhtiyaruddin Ghazi Shah](/wiki/Ikhtiyaruddin_Ghazi_Shah "Ikhtiyaruddin Ghazi Shah") of [Sonargaon](/wiki/Sonargaon "Sonargaon"); ultimately unifying Bengal into one single independent Sultanate. At its greatest extent, the Bengal Sultanate's realm and protectorates stretched from [Jaunpur](/wiki/Jaunpur,_Uttar_Pradesh "Jaunpur, Uttar Pradesh") in the west, [Tripura](/wiki/Tripura "Tripura") and [Arakan](/wiki/Arakan "Arakan") in the east, [Kamrup](/wiki/Kamarupa "Kamarupa") and [Kamata](/wiki/Kamata_Kingdom "Kamata Kingdom") in the north and [Puri](/wiki/Puri "Puri") in the south.

Although a [Sunni Muslim](/wiki/Sunni_Muslim "Sunni Muslim") monarchy ruled by [Turco-Persians](/wiki/Turco-Persian_tradition "Turco-Persian tradition"), [Bengalis](/wiki/Bengalis "Bengalis"), [Habshis](/wiki/Habshis "Habshis") and [Pashtuns](/wiki/Pashtuns "Pashtuns"), they still employed many non-Muslims in the administration and promoted a form of religious pluralism.[[25]](#cite_note-auto1-25)[[26]](#cite_note-26) It was known as one of the major trading nations of the medieval world, attracting immigrants and traders from different parts of the world.[[27]](#cite_note-eaton-27) Bengali ships and merchants traded across the region, including in Malacca, China, Africa, Europe and the Maldives through maritime links and overland trade routes. Contemporary European and Chinese visitors described Bengal as the "richest country to trade with" due to the abundance of goods in Bengal. In 1500, the royal capital of [Gaur](/wiki/Gauda_(city) "Gauda (city)") was the fifth-most populous city in the world with 200,000 residents.[[28]](#cite_note-28)[[29]](#cite_note-29)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3c/Adina_Mosque_at_Malda_district_of_West_Bengal_08.jpg/250px-Adina_Mosque_at_Malda_district_of_West_Bengal_08.jpg)](/wiki/File:Adina_Mosque_at_Malda_district_of_West_Bengal_08.jpg)

Ruins of the [Adina Mosque](/wiki/Adina_Mosque "Adina Mosque"), once the largest mosque in the [Indian subcontinent](/wiki/Indian_subcontinent "Indian subcontinent"), in [Pandua](/wiki/Pandua,_Malda "Pandua, Malda"), the first capital of the [Bengal Sultanate](/wiki/Bengal_Sultanate "Bengal Sultanate").

Persian was used as a diplomatic and commercial language. Arabic was the liturgical language of the clergy, and the [Bengali language](/wiki/Bengali_language "Bengali language") became a court language.[[30]](#cite_note-30) The patronage of the sultans raised Bengali from the language of the masses.[[31]](#cite_note-31) Sultan [Ghiyathuddin Azam Shah](/wiki/Ghiyathuddin_Azam_Shah "Ghiyathuddin Azam Shah") sponsored the construction of [madrasas](/wiki/Madrasa "Madrasa") in [Makkah](/wiki/Makkah "Makkah") and [Madinah](/wiki/Madinah "Madinah").[[32]](#cite_note-32) The schools became known as the [Ghiyasia Banjalia Madrasas](/wiki/Ghiyasia_Madrasa "Ghiyasia Madrasa"). [Taqi al-Din al-Fasi](/wiki/Taqi_al-Din_Muhammad_ibn_Ahmad_al-Fasi "Taqi al-Din Muhammad ibn Ahmad al-Fasi"), a contemporary Arab scholar, was a teacher at the madrasa in Makkah. The madrasa in Madinah was built at a place called Husn al-Atiq near the [Prophet's Mosque](/wiki/Prophet%27s_Mosque "Prophet's Mosque").[[33]](#cite_note-bpedia-33) Several other Bengali Sultans also sponsored madrasas in the [Hejaz](/wiki/Hejaz "Hejaz").[[34]](#cite_note-banglapedia.org1-34)

The [Karrani dynasty](/wiki/Karrani_dynasty "Karrani dynasty") was the last ruling dynasty of the sultanate. The Mughals became determined to bring an end to the independent kingdom. Mughal rule formally began with the [Battle of Rajmahal](/wiki/Battle_of_Rajmahal "Battle of Rajmahal") in 1576, when the last Sultan [Daud Khan Karrani](/wiki/Daud_Khan_Karrani "Daud Khan Karrani") was defeated by the forces of Emperor [Akbar](/wiki/Akbar "Akbar"), and the establishment of the [Bengal Subah](/wiki/Bengal_Subah "Bengal Subah"). The eastern deltaic [Bhati](/wiki/Bhati_(region) "Bhati (region)") region remained outside of Mughal control until being absorbed in the early 17th century. The delta was controlled by a confederation of aristocrats of the Sultanate, who became known as the [Baro-Bhuiyans](/wiki/Baro-Bhuiyan "Baro-Bhuiyan"). The Mughal government eventually suppressed the remnants of the Sultanate and brought all of Bengal under full Mughal control.

#### Jaunpur Sultanate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Muslim_period_in_the_Indian_subcontinent&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Jaunpur Sultanate")]

The [Jaunpur Sultanate](/wiki/Jaunpur_Sultanate "Jaunpur Sultanate") was founded in 1394 by Khwajah-i-Jahan Malik Sarwar, a eunuch slave and former [wazir](/wiki/Vizier "Vizier") of Sultan [Nasiruddin Muhammad Shah IV Tughluq](/wiki/Nasir_ud_din_Muhammad_Shah_III "Nasir ud din Muhammad Shah III"), amidst the disintegration of the Delhi Sultanate's [Tughlaq dynasty](/wiki/Tughlaq_dynasty "Tughlaq dynasty"). It was centred in [Jaunpur](/wiki/Jaunpur,_Uttar_Pradesh "Jaunpur, Uttar Pradesh"), and the Sultanate extended authority over [Awadh](/wiki/Awadh "Awadh") and a large part of the [Ganges](/wiki/Ganges "Ganges")-[Yamuna](/wiki/Yamuna "Yamuna") [Doab](/wiki/Doab "Doab") between 1394 and 1479. It reached its greatest height under the rule of Sultan Ibrahim Shah, who also vastly contributed to the development of Islamic education in the Sultanate. In 1479, Sultan Hussain Khan was defeated by the forces of [Bahlul Lodi](/wiki/Bahlul_Lodi "Bahlul Lodi"), Sultan of the [Lodi dynasty](/wiki/Lodi_dynasty "Lodi dynasty") of the [Delhi Sultanate](/wiki/Delhi_Sultanate "Delhi Sultanate"), which abruptly brought an end to independent Jaunpur and its reabsorption into the Delhi Sultanate.

#### Nawabs of Bengal

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Muslim_period_in_the_Indian_subcontinent&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Nawabs of Bengal")]

[Nawab](/wiki/Nawab "Nawab") was a title given by the Mughals to the governors of different provinces. During disintegration of the empire in the 18th century, many Nawabs became de facto independent.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cf/1751_map_of_India_from_%22Historical_Atlas_of_India%22%2C_by_Charles_Joppen.jpg/250px-1751_map_of_India_from_%22Historical_Atlas_of_India%22%2C_by_Charles_Joppen.jpg)](/wiki/File:1751_map_of_India_from_%22Historical_Atlas_of_India%22,_by_Charles_Joppen.jpg)

[Bengal Subah](/wiki/Bengal_Subah "Bengal Subah") in 1751 (in red). Muslim kingdoms of [Awadh](/wiki/Oudh_State "Oudh State"), [Hyderabad Deccan](/wiki/Hyderabad_state "Hyderabad state"), [Carnatic](/wiki/Carnatic_Sultanate "Carnatic Sultanate") and [Mysore](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Mysore "Kingdom of Mysore") can also be seen.

In the early 18th-century, the [Nawabs of Bengal and Murshidabad](/wiki/Nawabs_of_Bengal_and_Murshidabad "Nawabs of Bengal and Murshidabad") were the *de facto* independent ruler of the three regions of [Bengal](/wiki/Bengal "Bengal"), [Bihar](/wiki/Bihar "Bihar"), and [Orissa](/wiki/Orissa "Orissa") which constitute the modern-day sovereign country of [Bangladesh](/wiki/Bangladesh "Bangladesh") and the [Indian states](/wiki/Indian_state "Indian state") of [West Bengal](/wiki/West_Bengal "West Bengal"), [Bihar](/wiki/Bihar "Bihar") and [Orissa](/wiki/Orissa "Orissa").[[35]](#cite_note-35)[[36]](#cite_note-36) The Nawabs of Bengal oversaw a period of [proto-industrialization](/wiki/Proto-industrialization "Proto-industrialization"). The region was a major production center for cotton muslin cloth, silk cloth, shipbuilding, gunpowder, saltpetre, and metalworks. The British company eventually challenged the authority of the Nawabs. In the aftermath of the [siege of Calcutta](/wiki/Siege_of_Calcutta "Siege of Calcutta") in 1756, in which the Nawab's forces overran the main British base, the East India Company dispatched a fleet led by [Robert Clive](/wiki/Robert_Clive "Robert Clive") who defeated the last independent Nawab [Siraj-ud-Daulah](/wiki/Siraj-ud-Daulah "Siraj-ud-Daulah") at the [Battle of Plassey](/wiki/Battle_of_Plassey "Battle of Plassey") in 1757. [Mir Jafar](/wiki/Mir_Jafar "Mir Jafar") was installed as the puppet Nawab. His successor [Mir Qasim](/wiki/Mir_Qasim "Mir Qasim") attempted in vain to dislodge the British. The defeat of Nawab Mir Qasim of Bengal, Nawab [Shuja-ud-Daula](/wiki/Shuja-ud-Daula "Shuja-ud-Daula") of [Oudh](/wiki/Oudh "Oudh"), and Mughal Emperor [Shah Alam II](/wiki/Shah_Alam_II "Shah Alam II") at the [Battle of Buxar](/wiki/Battle_of_Buxar "Battle of Buxar") in 1764 paved the way for British expansion across India.

#### Nawabs of Awadh

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Muslim_period_in_the_Indian_subcontinent&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Nawabs of Awadh")]

[Nawab of Awadh](/wiki/Nawab_of_Awadh "Nawab of Awadh") ruled major parts of present-day [Uttar Pradesh](/wiki/Uttar_Pradesh "Uttar Pradesh"). The Nawabs of Awadh, along with many other [Nawabs](/wiki/Nawab "Nawab"), were regarded as members of the nobility of the greater Mughal Empire. They joined [Ahmad Shah Durrani](/wiki/Ahmad_Shah_Durrani "Ahmad Shah Durrani") during the [Third Battle of Panipat](/wiki/Third_Battle_of_Panipat "Third Battle of Panipat") (1761) and restored [Shah Alam II](/wiki/Shah_Alam_II "Shah Alam II") (r. 1760–1788 and 1788–1806) to the imperial throne. The Nawab of Awadh also fought the [Battle of Buxar](/wiki/Battle_of_Buxar "Battle of Buxar") (1764) preserving the interests of the Moghul. [Oudh State](/wiki/Oudh_State "Oudh State") eventually declared itself independent from the rule of the "Great Moghul" in 1818.[[37]](#cite_note-TOI-37)

Oudh joined other Indian states in an upheaval against British rule in 1858 during one of the last series of actions in the [Indian rebellion of 1857](/wiki/Indian_rebellion_of_1857 "Indian rebellion of 1857"). In the course of this uprising detachments of the British Indian Army from the [Bombay Presidency](/wiki/Bombay_Presidency "Bombay Presidency") overcame the disunited collection of Indian states in a single rapid campaign. Determined rebels continued to wage sporadic guerrilla clashes until the spring of 1859. This rebellion is also historically known as the *[Oudh campaign](/wiki/Oudh_campaign_of_1858-9 "Oudh campaign of 1858-9")*.[[38]](#cite_note-38)

#### Other Northern states

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Muslim_period_in_the_Indian_subcontinent&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Other Northern states")]

In northern India, the Multan-based [Langah Sultanate](/wiki/Langah_Sultanate "Langah Sultanate") and the [Kashmir Sultanate](/wiki/Kashmir_Sultanate "Kashmir Sultanate") were established during the 14th century. Nobles in the court of the Delhi Sultanate founded other Islamic dynasties elsewhere in India including [Khandesh Sultanate](/wiki/Khandesh_Sultanate "Khandesh Sultanate"). The [Kingdom of Rohilkhand](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Rohilkhand "Kingdom of Rohilkhand") was also a major power in northern India in the 18th century.

### South India

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Muslim_period_in_the_Indian_subcontinent&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: South India")]

Till the early 14th century, south India was ruled by Hindu dynasties. During the reign of [Sultan Alauddin Khalji](/wiki/Alauddin_Khalji "Alauddin Khalji") (r.1296–1316), his slave-general Malik Kafur led multiple campaigns to the south of the [Vindhyas](/wiki/Vindhyas "Vindhyas"), obtaining a considerable amount of wealth from [Devagiri](/wiki/Alauddin_Khalji%27s_conquest_of_Devagiri "Alauddin Khalji's conquest of Devagiri") (1308), [Warangal](/wiki/Siege_of_Warangal,_1310 "Siege of Warangal, 1310") (1310) and [Dwarasamudra](/wiki/Siege_of_Dwarasamudra "Siege of Dwarasamudra") (1311). These victories forced the [Yadava](/wiki/Yadavas_of_Devagiri "Yadavas of Devagiri") king [Ramachandra](/wiki/Ramachandra_of_Devagiri "Ramachandra of Devagiri"), the [Kakatiya](/wiki/Kakatiya "Kakatiya") king [Prataparudra](/wiki/Prataparudra "Prataparudra"), and the [Hoysala](/wiki/Hoysala "Hoysala") king [Ballala III](/wiki/Veera_Ballala_III "Veera Ballala III") to become Alauddin's [tributaries](/wiki/Tributary_state "Tributary state"). In 1321, [Muhammad bin Tughluq](/wiki/Muhammad_bin_Tughluq "Muhammad bin Tughluq") was sent by his father to the [Deccan Plateau](/wiki/Deccan_Plateau "Deccan Plateau") to fight a military campaign against the [Kakatiya dynasty](/wiki/Kakatiya_dynasty "Kakatiya dynasty"). In 1323, the future sultan successfully [laid siege upon the Kakatiya capital](/wiki/Siege_of_Warangal,_1323 "Siege of Warangal, 1323") in [Warangal](/wiki/Warangal "Warangal"). This victory over King [Prataparudra](/wiki/Prataparudra "Prataparudra") ended the [Kakatiya dynasty](/wiki/Kakatiya_dynasty "Kakatiya dynasty"). Although the control of Delhi sultanate was weakened after 1335 in the south, its successor Muslim states continued to rule Deccan plateau for next several centuries.

#### Bahmani Sultanate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Muslim_period_in_the_Indian_subcontinent&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Bahmani Sultanate")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/09/Bahamani-sultanate-map.svg/250px-Bahamani-sultanate-map.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Bahamani-sultanate-map.svg)

Bahmani sultanate in 1470

The Muhammad bin Tughlaq's failure to hold securely the Deccan and South India resulted in the rise of competing for Southern dynasties: the [Muslim](/wiki/Muslim "Muslim") [Bahmani Sultanate](/wiki/Bahmani_Sultanate "Bahmani Sultanate") (1347–1518) and the [Hindu](/wiki/Hindu "Hindu") [Vijayanagara Empire](/wiki/Vijayanagara_Empire "Vijayanagara Empire") (1336–1646). [Zafar Khan](/wiki/Hasan_Gangu "Hasan Gangu"), a former provincial governor under the Tughluqs, revolted against Delhi Sultans and proclaimed himself sultan, taking the title [Ala-ud-Din Bahman Shah](/wiki/Ala-ud-Din_Bahman_Shah "Ala-ud-Din Bahman Shah") in 1347. It was the first Muslim empire located in the [Deccan region](/wiki/Deccan_Plateau "Deccan Plateau").[[39]](#cite_note-39)[[40]](#cite_note-40) Bahmani empire was known for its perpetual wars with its rival [Vijayanagara](/wiki/Vijayanagara_Empire "Vijayanagara Empire"), which would outlast the Sultanate.[[41]](#cite_note-41) The Bahmani Sultans were patrons of the [Persian language](/wiki/Persian_language "Persian language"), [culture](/wiki/Culture_of_Iran "Culture of Iran") and [literature](/wiki/Persian_literature "Persian literature"), and some members of the dynasty became well-versed in that language and composed its literature in that language. The Bahmani Sultanate adopted the patterns established by the Delhi overlords in tax collection and administration, but its downfall was caused in large measure by the competition and hatred between Deccani (domiciled Muslim immigrants and local converts) and paradesi (foreigners or officials in temporary service). The Bahmani Sultanate initiated a process of cultural synthesis visible in [Hyderabad](/wiki/Hyderabad,_India "Hyderabad, India") where cultural flowering is still expressed in vigorous schools of Deccani architecture and painting. The later rulers are buried in an elaborate tomb complex, known as the [Bahmani Tombs](/wiki/Bahmani_Tombs "Bahmani Tombs").[[42]](#cite_note-Yazdani-42) The exterior of one of the tombs is decorated with coloured tiles. Arabic, Persian and Urdu inscriptions are inscribed inside the tombs.[[42]](#cite_note-Yazdani-42)[[43]](#cite_note-43)

#### Deccan sultanates

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Muslim_period_in_the_Indian_subcontinent&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Deccan sultanates")]

Main articles: [Bahmani Sultanate](/wiki/Bahmani_Sultanate "Bahmani Sultanate") and [Deccan sultanates](/wiki/Deccan_sultanates "Deccan sultanates")

The Bahmani Sultanate lasted for almost two centuries, until it fragmented into five smaller states, known as the [Deccan sultanates](/wiki/Deccan_sultanates "Deccan sultanates") ([Bijapur](/wiki/Bijapur_Sultanate "Bijapur Sultanate"), [Golconda](/wiki/Golconda_Sultanate "Golconda Sultanate"), [Ahmednagar](/wiki/Ahmednagar_Sultanate "Ahmednagar Sultanate"), [Berar](/wiki/Berar_sultanate "Berar sultanate"), and [Bidar](/wiki/Bidar_Sultanate "Bidar Sultanate")) in 1527. Although the five sultanates were all ruled by Muslims, their founders were of diverse, and often originally non-Muslim origins: the Ahmadnagar Sultanate was of Hindu-Brahmin origins; the Berar sultanate by a [Kannadiga Hindu](/wiki/Kannadiga "Kannadiga") convert; the Bidar Sultanate was founded by a Georgian slave; the Bijapur Sultanate was founded by a [Georgian](/wiki/Georgians "Georgians") slave purchased by [Mahmud Gawan](/wiki/Mahmud_Gawan "Mahmud Gawan") and the Golconda Sultanate was of [Turkmen](/wiki/Turkmens "Turkmens") origin.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e5/Battle_of_Talikota_%28formatted%29.jpg/250px-Battle_of_Talikota_%28formatted%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Battle_of_Talikota_(formatted).jpg)

Panorama depicting the [Battle of Talikota](/wiki/Battle_of_Talikota "Battle of Talikota") (1565), in which Deccan sultanates defeated the [Vijayanagara Empire](/wiki/Vijayanagara_Empire "Vijayanagara Empire"). *Ta'rif-i Husain Shahi* (Chronicle of Husain Shah).

The rulers of the Deccan sultanates made a number of cultural contributions in the fields of literature, art, architecture, and music. An important contribution was the development of the [Dakhani](/wiki/Dakhani "Dakhani") language, which, having started development under the [Bahamani](/wiki/Bahamani "Bahamani") rulers, developed into an independent spoken and literary language during this period by continuously borrowing from Arabic-Persian, Marathi, Kannada, and Telugu. Dakhani later became known as Dakhani Urdu to distinguish it from North Indian [Urdu](/wiki/Urdu "Urdu"). Deccani miniature painting—which flourished in the courts of Ahmadnagar, Bijapur, and Golconda—is another major cultural contribution of the Deccan sultanates.[[44]](#cite_note-44)

When the rulers of the five [Deccan sultanates](/wiki/Deccan_sultanates "Deccan sultanates") combined their forces and attacked the [Vijayanagara empire](/wiki/Vijayanagara_empire "Vijayanagara empire") in 1565, the empire crumbled at the [Battle of Talikot](/wiki/Battle_of_Talikot "Battle of Talikot").

#### Nizams of Hyderabad

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Muslim_period_in_the_Indian_subcontinent&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Nizams of Hyderabad")]

[Nizam](/wiki/Nizam "Nizam"), a shortened version of Nizam-ul-Mulk, meaning *Administrator of the Realm*, was the title of the native sovereigns of [Hyderabad state](/wiki/Hyderabad_state "Hyderabad state"), [India](/wiki/India "India"), since 1719, belonging to the [Asaf Jahi](/wiki/Asaf_Jah "Asaf Jah") dynasty. The dynasty was founded by [Mir Qamar-ud-Din Siddiqi](/wiki/Qamar-ud-din_Khan,_Asaf_Jah_I "Qamar-ud-din Khan, Asaf Jah I"), a [viceroy](/wiki/Viceroy "Viceroy") of the [Deccan](/wiki/Deccan_Plateau "Deccan Plateau") under the [Mughal emperors](/wiki/Mughal_Empire "Mughal Empire") from 1713 to 1721 who intermittently ruled under the title "Asaf Jah" in 1924. After [Aurangzeb's](/wiki/Aurangzeb "Aurangzeb") death in 1707, the Mughal Empire crumbled, and the viceroy in Hyderabad, the young Asaf Jah, declared..himself independent.  
The dynasty ruled for 7 generations, with the last Nizam – [Mir Osman Ali Khan](/wiki/Mir_Osman_Ali_Khan "Mir Osman Ali Khan") showing an enormous contributions on the field of education, [construction of major public buildings](/wiki/Mir_Osman_Ali_Khan#Construction_of_major_public_buildings "Mir Osman Ali Khan") across the kingdom, setting up of [Nizam's Guaranteed State Railway(NSGR)](/wiki/Nizam%27s_Guaranteed_State_Railway "Nizam's Guaranteed State Railway"), [donations to Universities, temples](/wiki/Mir_Osman_Ali_Khan#Philanthropy "Mir Osman Ali Khan")[[45]](#cite_note-45) and donating 14,000 acres (5,700 ha) of land from his personal estate to Vinobha Bhave's [Bhoodan movement](/wiki/Bhoodan_movement "Bhoodan movement").[[46]](#cite_note-46)

#### Mysore Kingdom

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Muslim_period_in_the_Indian_subcontinent&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Mysore Kingdom")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c4/Indian_Mysore_Kingdom_1784_map.svg/250px-Indian_Mysore_Kingdom_1784_map.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Indian_Mysore_Kingdom_1784_map.svg)

Mysore Kingdom in 1784.

[Hyder Ali](/wiki/Hyder_Ali "Hyder Ali") and [Tipu Sultan](/wiki/Tipu_Sultan "Tipu Sultan") held power and were de facto rulers of the [proto-industrialised](/wiki/Proto-industrialisation "Proto-industrialisation") [Mysore Sultanate](/wiki/Mysore_Sultanate "Mysore Sultanate") during the latter part of the 18th century. They made alliances with [France](/wiki/France "France") and fought the [Anglo-Mysore Wars](/wiki/Anglo-Mysore_Wars "Anglo-Mysore Wars") predominantly against the British.

#### Carnatic Sultanate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Muslim_period_in_the_Indian_subcontinent&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Carnatic Sultanate")]

The [Carnatic Sultanate](/wiki/Carnatic_Sultanate "Carnatic Sultanate") was a kingdom in [South India](/wiki/South_India "South India") between about 1690 and 1855, and was under the legal purview of the [Nizam of Hyderabad](/wiki/Nizam_of_Hyderabad "Nizam of Hyderabad"), until their demise.[[47]](#cite_note-47)[[48]](#cite_note-48) The Nawabs of Carnatic eventually ceded tax rights to the British in 1801 following [Carnatic wars](/wiki/Carnatic_wars "Carnatic wars"), and the kingdom was abolished.

#### Other Southern states

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Muslim_period_in_the_Indian_subcontinent&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Other Southern states")]

Other southern states include the [Arakkal Kingdom](/wiki/Arakkal_Kingdom "Arakkal Kingdom") (of modern-day Kerala) who were a subordinate of their masters the [Kolathiris](/wiki/Kolathiri "Kolathiri") and the short-lived [Madurai Sultanate](/wiki/Madurai_Sultanate "Madurai Sultanate") which was centered in and around Madurai and existed for barely 40 years.
